# Cultural Catalogue Audit

Generated by `backend/_catalogue_audit.py` against the live `_FAMILY_CATALOG` in `backend/dictionary/phrase_families.py` (451 total entries).

## Summary

- **Total English phrase family count:** 49
- **Detection rate on spot-test passage:** 1/8
- **Single biggest gap:** Catalogue size — English has under 500 entries, well below the 3,000-10,000 expected for a primary language; most everyday idioms a user pastes will not be in the catalogue at all, regardless of how the frontend displays matches.
- **Recommended next action:** Catalogue expansion is the priority. Source additional English entries from Wiktionary idiom categories, a licensed idioms dictionary, or LLM-assisted generation of variants for the existing entries, before investing further in frontend visibility polish.

## Query 1 — Total catalogue size by language

| Language | Code | Entries |
|---|---|---|
| Arabic | ar | 51 |
| Hebrew | he | 51 |
| Spanish | es | 50 |
| German | de | 50 |
| French | fr | 50 |
| Italian | it | 50 |
| Portuguese | pt | 50 |
| Russian | ru | 50 |
| English | en | 49 |

**Total across all languages: 451**

**Additional gap found while running this query:** `backend/nuance/` has plugin
infrastructure for 14 languages (ar, de, en, es, fr, grc, he, it, ja, ko, la,
pt, ru, zh), but the phrase-family catalogue only has entries for 9 of them
(en, es, fr, de, it, pt, ru, ar, he). **ja, ko, la, grc, and zh have zero
phrase_family catalogue entries** — users reading in those languages get no
idiom/expression detection at all, regardless of any frontend fix, because
there is nothing in `_FAMILY_CATALOG` for `match_phrase_families()` to find.

## Query 4 — Field completeness

**Note:** `PhraseFamily` (backend/dictionary/phrase_families.py) has no separate `etymology` field — etymological content is folded into `origin`. There is no distinct etymology object on this dataclass to measure, so that row is reported as N/A rather than fabricated. `match_type_note`/`confusables` are reported in its place as the closest comparable richness signals.

### English only

| Field | Coverage |
|---|---|
| origin text present | 100.0% |
| etymology object present | N/A — no such field on PhraseFamily; see note above |
| why_it_matters present | 12.2% |
| source_text present | 10.2% |
| tags present | 10.2% |
| confusables present | 4.1% |

### All languages combined

| Field | Coverage |
|---|---|
| origin text present | 98.0% |
| etymology object present | N/A — see note above |
| why_it_matters present | 31.3% |
| source_text present | 5.1% |
| tags present | 26.2% |
| confusables present | 3.1% |

## Query 5 — Spot-test on a known text

Passage parsed directly through `match_phrase_families()` (the same function the `en` nuance plugin calls), bypassing the frontend and the rest of the parse pipeline (tokenization here is a simple word/punctuation regex split, not the production spaCy tokenizer — close enough for surface-string matching fidelity, but flagging the difference rather than overclaiming full pipeline parity).

> It was raining cats and dogs. John bit the bullet and told his boss the project had gone pear-shaped. Every cloud has a silver lining, he supposed, but right now he couldn't see the wood for the trees. The writing was on the wall. He'd burned his bridges and now had to face the music.

| Expected idiom | Detected |
|---|---|
| raining cats and dogs | ✗ MISSED |
| bit the bullet | ✓ |
| gone pear-shaped | ✗ MISSED |
| every cloud has a silver lining | ✗ MISSED |
| see the wood for the trees | ✗ MISSED |
| the writing was on the wall | ✗ MISSED |
| burned his bridges | ✗ MISSED |
| face the music | ✗ MISSED |

**Score: 1/8**

Raw matches returned (surface span — family id — match type):

- "bit the bullet" — `bite_the_bullet` — inflectional_variant
